W2003922372: Vogt's landmark fate-mapping of the amphibian germ by local vital staining cites Przibram as a precedent for the very problem it solves more powerfully: marking identifiable points on the newt egg and following them into the embryo. Where Przibram used the egg's natural pigment 'zenith field' and artificial marks to infer the embryo's position on Triton alpestris, Vogt substitutes harmless dyes to trace the actual movements of cell material — the citation acknowledges the older marking method as the conceptual ancestor of his technique.
W1994455801: Campbell asks how the green hydra passes its symbiotic Chlorella to the next generation, tracking the algae as they move into the oocyte. He cites Hadzi's preliminary study as an early description of the hydra–alga partnership itself — the symbiosis whose cellular inheritance Campbell, eight decades later, works out in detail.
W1970687119: Ewer's taxonomic revision of the Hydridae — which dissolves the genus Pelmatohydra back into Hydra — leans on the early natural-history literature of the freshwater polyp. Hadzi's preliminary biological observations are cited among the foundational accounts that a modern classification of these animals has to reconcile.
W2911912236: Haug's study of the light reactions of hydras builds on the early behavioural and biological groundwork for the polyp; Hadzi's preliminary experiments are cited as part of that base of knowledge about how Hydra senses and responds to its surroundings.
W159867080: Beutler's experimental work on digestion in Hydra extends a question Hadzi had opened — how the polyp takes up and processes food — citing the BVA preliminary experiments as an early treatment of hydra feeding and intracellular digestion.
W2602302971: Arnold's comparative account of intracellular digestion in planarians sets Hadzi's hydra observations into a cross-phylum survey of how simple invertebrates digest within their cells, citing the BVA work as a comparative data point for the same digestive phenomenon in a cnidarian.
W2111213216: Muller's study of gonophore regeneration in hydroids, with attendant biological notes, cites Hadzi's preliminary hydra work within the broader biology of these freshwater cnidarians, drawing on it for baseline natural history of the polyps.
W2602691008: Marshall's observations on the behaviour and structure of Hydra continue the descriptive-experimental tradition that Hadzi's preliminary experiments exemplify, citing them among earlier accounts of how the polyp behaves and is built.
W2075746757: Kammerer — Hadzi's colleague at the BVA — reports another animal–alga symbiosis, between a dragonfly larva and a filamentous alga, and cites Hadzi's green-hydra observations as a parallel case of an animal–algal consortium, the very phenomenon Hadzi had described in the polyp.
W119508502: Buchner's synthesis of symbiosis research catalogues the green hydra among animal–alga partnerships, citing Hadzi's preliminary experiments as an early contribution to the study of intracellular algal symbiosis that Buchner systematized.
W2036516030: Koelitz's morphological and experimental investigations of Hydra build directly on the experimental program Hadzi's preliminary study opened, citing it as a point of departure for systematic work on the polyp.
W2021123969: Rehm's study of 'depression' and reduction — de-growth — in Hydra draws on the foundational biology of the polyp that Hadzi surveyed, citing the BVA preliminary experiments among early accounts of the animal's plastic response to conditions.
W2091145767: A continuation of Muller's hydroid gonophore-regeneration observations, again citing Hadzi's preliminary hydra biology as part of the descriptive basis for work on these colonial cnidarians.
W2036902491: Goetsch ties the green hydra's algal symbiosis to the question of species boundaries, citing Hadzi's early symbiosis observations as evidence in the debate over whether symbiotic green hydras and brown hydras are distinct species.
W4234357049: The continued part of Koelitz's morphological–experimental hydra studies, again grounded on Hadzi's preliminary program as a starting point for experimental work on the polyp.
W2006096358: Steche's review of the state of knowledge on the freshwater polyp folds Hadzi's preliminary experiments into a 1908 synthesis of what was then known about Hydra's biology.
W2217287367: Buddenbrock's comparative treatise on the invertebrate phyla cites Hadzi when treating nutrition and symbiosis in the cnidarians, using the green-hydra case as a textbook example of an animal–algal feeding relationship.
W2484693511: Przibram's own Experimental-Zoologie folds Hadzi's BVA study into the institute's programmatic survey of experimental zoology — a contemporary, in-house reference rather than an independent modern engagement, which is why it reads as historiographic.
W4244902633: A 1963 bibliography in which Hadzi's paper appears as a reference entry, listed rather than substantively engaged.
W2075657090: Gliboff's history of Kammerer's experimental practice reads this paper as a centrepiece of his program to mould organisms through environment and heredity, citing it to reconstruct what Kammerer claimed and how he worked — a historiographic use, not a test of the biology.
W2022754048: Witschi's experimental study of gonad development in the grass frog engages Kammerer's claims about environmentally enforced reproductive change, citing the Alytes work as a contemporary attempt to show heritable modification of reproductive structures in amphibians.
W1981194605: Gliboff's book-length treatment cites the Alytes paper as a key document for reconstructing Kammerer's neo-Lamarckian experiments and their early-twentieth-century scientific context.
W2546244624: Vargas reinterprets Kammerer's midwife-toad results through modern epigenetics, citing this paper as the primary report of the inherited, environmentally induced traits he argues could reflect epigenetic rather than strictly Lamarckian inheritance — a striking late re-engagement with the original claim.
W2892829925: Meyer's historical overview of epigenetics places Kammerer's enforced-adaptation experiments in the prehistory of the field, citing the Alytes work as an early claim of environmentally induced heritable change later reframed in epigenetic terms.
W2326876567: Kammerer's own later experiments on the olm (Proteus anguinus) extend this program, citing the 1909 paper as the methodological precedent for inducing and then inheriting reproductive and developmental modifications in cave-dwelling amphibians.
W2099717200: Gliboff's rebuttal of the epigenetic reinterpretation returns to this original article to argue that Kammerer's own descriptions do not support the modern reading — using the paper as primary evidence in a historiographic dispute over what he actually demonstrated.
W2050835603: Weissmann's essay revisits the midwife-toad affair, citing the paper amid the question of whether Kammerer's reported inheritance was epigenetics before its time or, instead, a matter of deception.
W4400063313: Muller's conceptual analysis of competing models of inheritance cites Kammerer's enforced-adaptation experiments as a historical instance of the 'inclusive' view that environment can write heritable change, set against the gene-centric account.
W3161558943: Nahm's reappraisal re-examines Kammerer's experiments in light of epigenetics and epigenetic inheritance, citing this paper as a core record of the disputed results he reassesses.
W2028792703: Logan's history of 'organic memory' situates Kammerer's inheritance of enforced adaptations within early-twentieth-century attempts to cast acquired characters as a kind of biological memory, citing the paper as an exemplar of that program.
W2616532932: Kammerer's companion study on enforced colour changes is the next instalment of the same series, citing this reproductive-adaptation paper as the precedent in his sequence of works on inherited, environmentally enforced modifications.
W2327131916: Another instalment in Kammerer's 'enforced changes' series, this one on form, citing the 1909 reproductive-adaptation paper as the foundational case of the inheritance he claims to extend to morphology.
W1546740169: Logan and Brauckmann's institutional history of the BVA cites Kammerer's Alytes work as emblematic of the institute's project — maintaining organisms in captivity to integrate development, environment and heredity — using it to characterize the laboratory's distinctive experimental culture.
W4210998716: Arntzen revisits the midwife-toad controversy from the standpoint of modern evolutionary biology, citing this paper as the original claim whose status — contentious but, he argues, not fully closed — he re-evaluates half a century on.
W2795442749: Gliboff's 'cold case reopened' returns once more to the Kammerer affair, citing the Alytes paper as the contested evidence at the centre of the reopened inquiry.
W2922657920: Uhlenhuth's work on the synchronized metamorphosis of transplanted salamander eyes cites Kammerer's enforced-adaptation experiments as a contemporary demonstration that external conditions can steer the developmental and reproductive timing of amphibians.
W1975527077: Ekman's account of species formation in the copepod Limnocalanus through the accumulated long-range effect of an environmental change cites Kammerer's enforced reproductive adaptations as a parallel case for the heritability of milieu-induced change across generations.
W1971806644: Schiller's preliminary experiments on the inheritance of acquired characters cite Kammerer's Alytes work as a leading contemporary claim of such inheritance, against which Schiller frames his own tests.
W4211053762: Gates's survey of mutation and evolution cites Kammerer's enforced-adaptation experiments while weighing the evidence for and against the inheritance of acquired characters in the post-Mendelian debate.
W4240158437: This 1911 review of heredity cites Kammerer's reproductive-adaptation experiments among the recent results then reshaping the debate over whether acquired characters could be inherited.
W2141724773: A second indexed instance of Gates's mutation-and-evolution survey, again citing the Alytes work within the acquired-characters debate.
W959809029: This modern natural-history synthesis of the common midwife toad cites Kammerer's work as part of the species' research history — the same organism, Alytes obstetricans, still studied today, now for its ecology and conservation rather than its contested inheritance.
